title: Foot Soldiers of Financialization
author: https://hedgehogreview.com/contributors/chris-arnade
text:
I met Chrome Dome, a retired trucker wearing a red MAGA hat over his bald head, in a Lewiston, Maine, McDonald’s three weeks before Trump was first elected. During the previous two years, I had interviewed thousands of people like him across the United States, in towns that, like Lewiston, were dealing with industrial decline and loss. I was pretty sure of what was about to come, down to the details—a soliloquy on decline, of good jobs moved to Mexico or China, of safe, clean neighborhoods destroyed by immigrants, of politicians who cared only about big-city billionaires and professors, not hard-working people, and then maybe a rant about Clinton (one or both), Obama, Reagan, or Bush (one or both), in that order of frequency.

Chrome Dome, in his early eighties, delivered it all with the usual color. Both of his parents had worked in textile mills that closed in the 1990s, he had a good childhood despite being poor because “we never wanted for anything,” and he had fashioned a good life for himself as a trucker, eventually moving into the house he was raised in and starting his own family. But all that had changed, and now his grandkids had no chance unless they left town and went to college and never came back, which is what he hoped they would do—because why would you stay in this shithole? And, of course, none of this was fair, and that was why he was voting for Trump. No, he didn’t usually vote, couldn’t remember the last time he had, because what was the point? Weren’t they all the same, out only for their billionaire friends? Sure, Trump was a billionaire, but not like the other ones, because he says things you aren’t supposed to say, and that gets everybody’s panties all twisted in a knot.

When I ask him when it all “went wrong,” he launches into a critique of the trucking industry, complains about the decline in lifestyle and wages over his life, and concludes by saying, “And that is the last time I voted for a president, because he stabbed us working people in the back.” “Reagan?” I ask. “No, Jimmy Carter.” And that is when I learn, to my embarrassment, that it was Carter, the candidate I had once campaigned for at my parents’ side, who had deregulated the trucking industry. 

That move was part of a larger program of deregulation that would reshape the country, triggering a deluge of consolidation, offshoring, and financialization. While this eroded America’s industrial base, especially in the Midwest, it engorged Wall Street with profits and filled New York City with young, highly educated professionals who would remake it along with the nation, and then result, arguably, four decades later, in Chrome Dome, like a lot of other “low-educational attainment” Americans, voting for and electing Donald Trump, to the surprise of everyone with a national voice.

Yuppies, a new book by historian Dylan Gottlieb, is an attempt to portray how sweeping a national transformation this set of policies (deregulation, financialization, deindustrialization) represented, by focusing on its foot soldiers in New York City: the meritocratic class of strivers who arrived in vast numbers beginning in the late 1970s. 

They were the top graduates of the finest colleges, drawn to New York City by extraordinarily high-paying jobs in companies that were anything but antiestablishment—bulge-bracket banks, global accounting firms, and the law firms that defended and supported both. They were living proof that whatever idealism the prior decade might have inspired, plenty of its members could still be enticed with a large and steady paycheck from a corporate America that soon manufactured little that was tangible, besides enormous profits.

Over the next decade, these men and women changed New York City at all levels—economically, demographically, culturally, and politically—reinvigorating a broken, crime-ridden city with the profits that derived from (depending on your perspective) either a transformational wave of productivity, efficiency, and economic growth, or from the strip-mining of working-class America, taking away all its people held sacred and replacing it with more stuff at lower costs.  

I was, and am, a member of this group, although I never saw myself as one. Yuppie was a dirty word when I was in college. Yet cultural forces have a way of shaping us despite our best intentions, and, six months after receiving a PhD in physics, I joined a booming Wall Street, walking a path blazed fifteen years earlier by the yuppies. My mother, a lifelong Democrat, never approved: The banks were not to be trusted, ever. They had foreclosed on her family dairy farm back in the 1930s, sending her, her brother, and her widowed mother into a decade of penury—and a lifetime of voting for the firmly anti-banking, working-class party, the Democrats.

I arrived on a Wall Street replete with new employees who looked nothing like me but thought like me. We were a diverse mix drawn from every part of the United States—from small Southern towns, from the Midwest, even from the trailer parks of Appalachia—all of us highly educated and sharing a philosophy best described as “libertarianism lite.” This was, uniformly, a socially liberal, fiscally conservative group who checked all the right diversity boxes and believed that individual liberty, especially when applied to businesses, was collectively beneficial and even represented history’s natural endpoint.

This philosophy was incarnate in newly elected President Bill Clinton, who was one of us. He might have acted more down-home than we did, but what mattered was that he had become the leader of the free world on merit alone. He represented the new Democratic Party, one that embraced Wall Street instead of shunning it, that was committed to undoing the bad deeds Franklin D. Roosevelt had done—all things my mother and her generation believed needed to be done to keep the banks from ravaging the country. That included bailing out banks when they found themselves out over their skis and ripping up the last of the Depression-era financial regulations, such as the Glass-Steagall Act, already a dead letter.

After twenty years and a few more bailouts, I quit Wall Street to focus on writing the stories of the rest of America—not only the parts we literally flew over but also neighborhoods and communities in New York City and New Jersey just miles away from the country’s financial center. I soon saw, and heard, that contrary to what our spreadsheets said, people who lived in these places hadn’t necessarily benefited from the new two-party political consensus that financialization was good, borders should be permeable (especially to trade), and that “Made in the USA” was an outdated sentiment—provincial, emotional, and economically inefficient.

After seven years of reporting, I coined the terms front-row and back-row to explain the contrast I saw between my Wall Street life and my reporting—two ethnographic types, divided by education, that I believed explained the largest cultural, moral, and political differences in our country. I was front-row; Chrome Dome and everyone like him I’d spoken to were back-row.

Reading Dylan Gottlieb’s book, I now see the yuppies as front-row America in its larval stage, an idea I had intuited both from my own life and from other reading but hadn’t fully comprehended. It is a detailed, well-researched book that substantiates the yuppie caricature while refusing to treat the actual individuals as cartoonishly evil or self-obsessed. 

Gottlieb’s observations on the reshaping of New York City’s culture, especially its exploding gourmet food scene, does the best job of navigating this tension. It follows the career of corporate lawyers Tim and Nina Zagat, who turned their hobby into the ubiquitous red restaurant guide that became the yuppies’ culinary bible. As Gottlieb writes, “The story…exposes the two seemingly contradictory tendencies embodied by yuppie gourmet culture: one toward democratization and one toward distinction.” That sentiment also describes the yuppie project writ large.

Yet Gottlieb gives the yuppies far more agency than the rest of the players in the drama of America in the 1980s. Lower-income residents of New York City–area neighborhoods like the Upper West Side and Hoboken, New Jersey, whom yuppies ultimately displaced, take the role of helpless victims.

On gentrification, Gottlieb’s writing is forceful, and his views emerge strongly—namely, that large parts of the yuppification process were decidedly negative, especially the displacement of poorer, largely minority citizens by means of eviction and even arson.

While I am certainly sympathetic to parts of this view, it’s hard for me to be too romantic about New York City during the 1960s and 1970s, a city some claimed harbored one-quarter of the nation’s addicts on the Upper West Side alone—all while Hoboken, once a functional, working city, won the dubious honor of being home to New Jersey’s second-highest percentage of people on welfare.

Gentrification didn’t happen without reason—and white flight, a key factor in the decline of New York City, wasn’t caused by the whims of whites but rather took place because their urban neighborhoods had descended into drugs, despair, and crime, partly due to the dramatic social changes of the civil rights movement but also to the disappearance of jobs that had once supported them, jobs that didn’t require a college degree, had departed for the suburbs, or were eliminated due to consolidation, offshoring, or technological change. 

The American economy was being reshaped—and the meritocratic, highly credentialed class and industries represented by the yuppies, whose work was intellectual rather than physical, were the winners, at the expense of everyone else. But why? Why did the supposedly idealistic 1960s end up launching such a drastic and inequitable transformation of the US? Yuppies doesn’t address that question in depth; it would require a book of its own. There is a nod toward the impact of civil rights and the idea that yuppies were a natural emanation from the movement—an elite class defined not by the outdated and prior anti-liberal forms of status (family, race, ethnicity, and gender) but by merit alone. There is also a nod toward the idea that it was the result of the anti-institutional worldview of the 1960s, a disgust with politics, government, and labor unions—an individualistic, antiestablishment cynicism that drew them from Harvard Law School, ironically, into Wall Street law firms, where they could at least find “individual actualization in the capital markets.” But a finer-grained sense of how that happened is lacking.

The most interesting section deals with the political ramifications of this social transformation, manifest in the rise of Gary Hart, the charismatic young senator from Colorado who challenged Walter Mondale in the 1984 Democratic primary. This was a fight between the new party of the yuppies and their economic wonks and the old party of the working class and their trade unions. Hart might have lost the primary, but more than four decades later, the change he represented is close to complete, with the transformation of the Democratic Party into one dominated by educated elites. 

Trump is mentioned only briefly in Yuppies, in the epilogue, and I doubt that either the author or his readers would like to connect the rise of the yuppies to the Trumpian platform of discontent with the dominant liberal economic orthodoxy and social conventions, even though the linkage is obvious. By the time I met Chrome Dome, I was constantly telling my readers, to the ridicule of almost everyone, that Donald Trump could win the 2016 election because the back-row (those displaced by the yuppies and their policies) felt they had nowhere else to go. To them, the Democrats were now no different than the pre-Trump Republicans. 

The two presumptive nominees heading into that 2016 election, Jeb Bush and Hillary Clinton, were both about as yuppie-coded as could be, one coming from the old-money Establishment, the other from the new meritocracy. To pundits, they represented a deepening partisan divide—but to those outside the policy class, they represented two sides of the same out-of-touch, urban-elite coin, united in their belief that it was the role of their class to craft policy that was best for America. And that meant what was best for Wall Street, best for New York City, and, most of all, best for the rest of their meritocratic class. 

Ten years later, with Trump elected again, to the continued confusion and irritation of the front-row, Yuppies is helpful reading for those who want to understand how we got here. We still live in the nation that the yuppies built, to the enduring chagrin of a majority of their fellow citizens.
